summary: Charity Majors reflects on how her views on AI have shifted dramatically in the year since she co-keynoted SRECon25, where she urged skeptical SREs to engage with AI without being reflexively antagonistic. She now believes the center of gravity has fully shifted to AI/agentic workflows and advocates for engineers to proactively embrace this change rather than wait for it to be forced on them. SREs in particular, with their outcome-orientation and experience building guardrails, are well-positioned to lead in this new era.
key_points: Skepticism about AI was reasonable in early 2025, but the bit has flipped — AI-generated code has moved past the 'slop phase' and the agentic revolution is genuinely underway
Engineers should proactively swim toward the AI wave rather than waiting for it to crash over them, as career risk for laggards is high
Self-awareness about cognitive biases (confirmation bias, reflexive pessimism or optimism) is critical for navigating a high-uncertainty moment
SREs' outcome-orientation and experience building guardrails makes them uniquely suited to thrive and lead in an AI-driven engineering world
AGI remains speculative — extraordinary claims still require extraordinary evidence, and tech breakthroughs historically plateau after early disruption
money_quotes: Don't wait for it to be forced on you. Swim out to meet it.
That toddler is heading off to school. With a loaded gun.
Sometimes the hype train brings you internets, sometimes the hype train brings you tulips.
The industry needs our skills to help engineering teams go fast forever.
Know your nature, and lean against it.
key_insight: SREs' career-long focus on outcomes over craft makes them uniquely positioned to lead — not resist — the AI transition, but only if they proactively engage rather than wait for change to be forced on them.
